subject: Physics
q: A cylinder of fixed capacity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>44</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>8</mn></math> litres contains helium gas at standard temperature and pressure. The amount of heat needed to raise the temperature of gas in the cylinder by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> will be (Given gas constant <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>8</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
question: A cylinder of fixed capacity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>44</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>8</mn></math> litres contains helium gas at standard temperature and pressure. The amount of heat needed to raise the temperature of gas in the cylinder by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> will be (Given gas constant <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>8</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
solution:
At STP volume occupied by one mole of a gas is 22.4 L. Therefore, number of moles are,  n=44.822.4=2 mol.

The process is isochoric process as the volume of the cylinder remains fixed and helium is a monatomic gas. Therefore, amount of heat needed will be,

Q=nCvΔT=232R20=3×8.3×20=498 J

subject: Physics
q:
A water heater of power 2000 W is used to heat water. The specific heat capacity of water is 4200 J kg-1 K-1. The efficiency of heater is 70%. Time required to heat 2 kg of water from 10°C to 60°C is _____ s.

(Assume that the specific heat capacity of water remains constant over the temperature range of the water).
question:
A water heater of power 2000 W is used to heat water. The specific heat capacity of water is 4200 J kg-1 K-1. The efficiency of heater is 70%. Time required to heat 2 kg of water from 10°C to 60°C is _____ s.

(Assume that the specific heat capacity of water remains constant over the temperature range of the water).
solution:
Heat provided to water will be responsible for increase in temperature. Therefore, we can write

η×P×t=M×s×ΔT

Now, η=70100=0.7 and energy=power×time.

Therefore,

t=2×4200×60°-10°0.7×2000 s=300 s

subject: Physics
q: The thermistors are usually made of
question: The thermistors are usually made of
options: metals with low temperature coefficient of resistivity
metals with high temperature coefficient of resistivity
metal oxides with high temperature coefficient of resistivity "
semiconducting materials having low temperature coefficient of resistivity.
solution:
These are devices whose resistance varies quite markedly with temperature mean having high-temperature coefficient of resistivity. [Their name are derived from thermal resistors]. Depending on their composition they can have either negative temperature coefficient or positive temperature coefficient or positive temperature coefficient or positive temperature coefficient characteristics.
The negative temperature coefficient type consists of a mixture of oxides of iron, nickel, and cobalt with small amounts of other substances. The positive temperature coefficient types are based on barium titanate.

subject: Physics
q:
A lead bullet penetrates into a solid object and melts. Assuming that 40% of its kinetic energy is used to heat it, the initial speed of bullet is
(Given, initial temperature of the bullet =127°C,
Melting point of the bullet =327°C,
Latent heat of fusion of lead =2.5×104 J kg-1,
Specific heat capacity of lead =125 J kg K-1)
question:
A lead bullet penetrates into a solid object and melts. Assuming that 40% of its kinetic energy is used to heat it, the initial speed of bullet is
(Given, initial temperature of the bullet =127°C,
Melting point of the bullet =327°C,
Latent heat of fusion of lead =2.5×104 J kg-1,
Specific heat capacity of lead =125 J kg K-1)
solution:
Loss of kinetic energy of the lead bullet=Heat generated to raise its temperature from 127 °C to 327 °C.

40 % loss of the kinetic energy of the lead bullet=Heat gained by the lead bullet to raise its temperature.

0.412mv2=m×SPb×∆T+m×LPb

0.4×12mv2=mSPbT2-t1+mLPb

0.2mv2=m×125×327-127+2.5×104

v2=5×125×200+2.5×104

v2=5×25000+25000

v=500 m s-1

subject: Physics
q: Water of volume 2 L in a closed container is heated with a coil of 1 kW. While water is heated, the container loses energy at a rate of 160 J/s. In how much time will the temperature of water rise from 27<sup>o</sup>C to 77<sup>o</sup>C ? (Specific heat of water is 4.2 kJ/kg and that of the container is negligible).
question: Water of volume 2 L in a closed container is heated with a coil of 1 kW. While water is heated, the container loses energy at a rate of 160 J/s. In how much time will the temperature of water rise from 27<sup>o</sup>C to 77<sup>o</sup>C ? (Specific heat of water is 4.2 kJ/kg and that of the container is negligible).
solution:
P = 1 0 0 0  watt

∴   Pin=1000-160 J/s=840 J/s

Now,   d Q dt = ms dT dt

∴    840=msdTdt

∴    dt = ms 8 4 0 dT

Integrating both sides we have;

t = ms 8 4 0 ∫ dT

    = 2 × 1 0 0 0 × 1 0 - 6 × 1 0 0 0 × 4.2 × 1 0 0 0 8 4 0 × 5 0

    = 5 0 0 sec

    = 8  min 2 0 sec

subject: Physics
q: A steel rod of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and cross-sectional area <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> is heated from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> without being allowed to extend or bend. The compressive tension produced in the rod is _____<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>4</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math>. (Given Young's modulus of steel <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>11</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>, coefficient of linear expansion <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> )
question: A steel rod of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and cross-sectional area <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> is heated from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> without being allowed to extend or bend. The compressive tension produced in the rod is _____<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>4</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math>. (Given Young's modulus of steel <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>11</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>, coefficient of linear expansion <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> )
solution:
The formula to calculate the Young's modulus of the material of the wire is given by

Y=FLA∆L   ...1

The increase in length of the wire due to increase in temperature is given by

∆L=LαT2-T1   ...2

Substitute the expression for the extension in length from equation (2) into equation (1) and simplify to obtain the required compressive tension.

Y=FLALαT2-T1⇒F=AαYT2-T1   ...3

Substitute the values of the known parameters into equation (3) to calculate the required compressive tension in the wire.

F=10-4 m2×10-5 K-1×2×1011 Nm-2×200° C-0° C= 4×104 N

subject: Physics
q: Two conductors have the same resistance at $0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ but their temperature coefficients of resistance are $\alpha_1$ and $\alpha_2$. The respective temperature coefficients of their series and parallel combinations are nearly
question: Two conductors have the same resistance at $0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ but their temperature coefficients of resistance are $\alpha_1$ and $\alpha_2$. The respective temperature coefficients of their series and parallel combinations are nearly
solution:
Let $R_0$ be the initial resistance of both conductors $\therefore \quad$ At temperature $\theta$ their resistance will be, $R_1=R_0\left(1+\alpha_1 \theta\right)$ and $R_2=R_0\left(1+\alpha_2 \theta\right)$
for, series combination, $\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{s}}=\mathrm{R}_1+\mathrm{R}_2$ $R_{s 0}\left(1+\alpha_s \theta\right)=R_0\left(1+\alpha_1 \theta\right)+R_0\left(1+\alpha_2 \theta\right)$
where $\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{s} 0}=\mathrm{R}_0+\mathrm{R}_0=2 \mathrm{R}_0$
$\therefore \quad 2 \mathrm{R}_0\left(1+\alpha_s \theta\right)=2 \mathrm{R}_0+\mathrm{R}_0 \theta\left(\alpha_1+\alpha_2\right)$
or $\quad \alpha_s=\frac{\alpha_1+\alpha_2}{2}$
for parallel combination,
$$
R_p=\frac{R_1 R_2}{R_1+R_2}
$$
where, $R_{p 0}=\frac{R_0 R_0}{R_0+R_0}=\frac{R_0}{2}$
$\therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{R}_0}{2}\left(1+\alpha_{\mathrm{p}} \theta\right)=\frac{\mathrm{R}_0^2\left(1+\alpha_1 \theta+\alpha_2 \theta+\alpha_1 \alpha_2 \theta\right)}{\mathrm{R}_0\left(2+\alpha_1 \theta+\alpha_2 \theta\right)}$
as $\alpha_1$ and $\alpha_2$ are small quantities
$\therefore \quad \alpha_1 \alpha_2$ is negligible
or $\quad \alpha_{\mathrm{p}}=\frac{\alpha_1+\alpha_2}{2+\left(\alpha_1+\alpha_2\right) \theta}=\frac{\alpha_1+\alpha_2}{2}\left[1-\left(\alpha_1+\alpha_2\right) \theta\right]$
as $\left(\alpha_1+\alpha_2\right)^2$ is negligible
$\therefore \quad \alpha_p=\frac{\alpha_1+\alpha_2}{2}$

subject: Physics
q: A body cools in 7 minutes from 60oC to 40oC. The temperature of the surrounding is 10oC. The temperature of the body after the next 7 minutes will be
question: A body cools in 7 minutes from 60oC to 40oC. The temperature of the surrounding is 10oC. The temperature of the body after the next 7 minutes will be
solution:
Newton's law of cooling states that 

T1-T2t=KT1+T22-T0   ...(i)

where T0 is the temperature of the surrounding. From equation (i),

60-407×60=K60+402-10

⇒207×60=40K⇒K=207×60×40

Let the temperature of the body after the given time be x °C. By Newton's law of cooling,

40-x7×60=K40+x2-10⇒40-x7×60=114×60(20+x2)⇒160-4x=20+x⇒x=28 °C

subject: Physics
q: The resistances of the platinum wire of a platinum resistance thermometer at the ice point and steam point are $8 \Omega$ and $10 \Omega$ respectively. After inserting in a hot bath of temperature $400^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, the resistance of platinum wire is :
question: The resistances of the platinum wire of a platinum resistance thermometer at the ice point and steam point are $8 \Omega$ and $10 \Omega$ respectively. After inserting in a hot bath of temperature $400^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, the resistance of platinum wire is :
solution:
Given $R_0=8 \Omega, R_{100}=10 \Omega$ $\therefore \mathrm{R}_{100}=\mathrm{R}_0(1+\alpha \Delta \mathrm{T})$
Also, $R_{400}=R_0\left(1+\alpha \Delta \mathrm{T}^1\right)$ $\begin{aligned} & \therefore 10=8(1+\alpha \times 100) \Rightarrow 100 \alpha=\frac{1}{4} \\ & \therefore \mathrm{R}_{400}=8(1+400 \alpha)=8(1+1)=16 \Omega \end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: Two plates A and B have thermal conductivities <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>84</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>126</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> respectively. They have same surface area and same thickness. They are placed in contact along their surfaces. If the temperatures of the outer surfaces of  A and B are kept at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>100</mn><mo>∘</mo></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>0</mn><mo>∘</mo></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> respectively, then the temperature of the surface of contact in steady state is_____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mmultiscripts><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mprescripts></mprescripts><none></none><mo>∘</mo></mmultiscripts></math>.
question: Two plates A and B have thermal conductivities <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>84</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>126</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> respectively. They have same surface area and same thickness. They are placed in contact along their surfaces. If the temperatures of the outer surfaces of  A and B are kept at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>100</mn><mo>∘</mo></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>0</mn><mo>∘</mo></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> respectively, then the temperature of the surface of contact in steady state is_____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mmultiscripts><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mprescripts></mprescripts><none></none><mo>∘</mo></mmultiscripts></math>.
solution:
Let the temperature of the contact surface be T,

So, HA=HB

KAA'(TA-T)L=KBA'(T-TB)L⇒84(100-T)=126(T-0)⇒2(100-T)=3T⇒T=40 °C

subject: Physics
q: Two metallic blocks M1 and M2 of same area of cross-section are connected to each other (as shown in figure). If the thermal conductivity of M2 is K then the thermal conductivity of M1 will be : [Assume steady state heat conduction]
question: Two metallic blocks M1 and M2 of same area of cross-section are connected to each other (as shown in figure). If the thermal conductivity of M2 is K then the thermal conductivity of M1 will be : [Assume steady state heat conduction]
solution:
Let thermal conductivity of M1 be K'.

Using heat current relation for steady state conduction,

K'A100-8016=KA80-08

⇒K'×2016=K×808⇒K'=8K

subject: Physics
q: A thermometer graduated according to a linear scale reads a value $x_{0}$ when in contact with boiling water, and $x_{0} / 3$ when in contact with ice. What is the temperature of an object in ${ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, if this thermometer in the contact with the object reads $x_{0} / 2 ?$
question: A thermometer graduated according to a linear scale reads a value $x_{0}$ when in contact with boiling water, and $x_{0} / 3$ when in contact with ice. What is the temperature of an object in ${ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, if this thermometer in the contact with the object reads $x_{0} / 2 ?$
solution:
Let required temperature $=\mathrm{T}^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{T}^{\circ} \mathrm{C}=\frac{\mathrm{x}_{0}}{2}-\frac{\mathrm{x}_{0}}{3}=\frac{\mathrm{x}_{0}}{6}$ $\&\left(\mathrm{x}_{0}-\frac{\mathrm{x}_{0}}{3}\right)=\left(100-0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right)$ $\Rightarrow \frac{2 \mathrm{x}_{0}}{3}=100 \Rightarrow \mathrm{x}_{0}=\frac{300}{2}$ $\Rightarrow \mathrm{T}^{\circ} \mathrm{C}=\frac{\mathrm{x}_{0}}{6}=\frac{150}{6}=25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$

subject: Physics
q: $100 \mathrm{~g}$ of water is heated from $30^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $50^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Ignoring the slight expansion of the water, the change in its internal energy is (specific heat of water is $4148 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{kg} / \mathrm{K}$ ):
question: $100 \mathrm{~g}$ of water is heated from $30^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $50^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Ignoring the slight expansion of the water, the change in its internal energy is (specific heat of water is $4148 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{kg} / \mathrm{K}$ ):
solution:
$\Delta \mathrm{Q}=\Delta \mathrm{U}+\Delta \mathrm{W}$ (ignoring expansion)
$\Delta \mathrm{U}=\mathrm{ms} \Delta \mathrm{T}=0.1 \times 4.184 \times 20=8.368 \mathrm{~kJ}$

subject: Physics
q: An ice cube of dimensions <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mo>×</mo><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mo>×</mo><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> is placed in an insulation box of wall thickness <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. The box keeping the ice cube at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> of temperature is brought to a room of temperature <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>. The rate of melting of ice is approximately: (Latent heat of fusion of ice is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>5</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> and thermal conducting of insulation wall is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>05</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mo>°</mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
question: An ice cube of dimensions <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mo>×</mo><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mo>×</mo><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> is placed in an insulation box of wall thickness <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. The box keeping the ice cube at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> of temperature is brought to a room of temperature <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>. The rate of melting of ice is approximately: (Latent heat of fusion of ice is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>5</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> and thermal conducting of insulation wall is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>05</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mo>°</mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
solution:
Using the equation of conduction,

dQdt=KAΔTl

Now the total area of the box will be,

A=20.6×0.5+0.5×0.2+0.2×0.6

A=20.3+0.1+0.12

A=20.52=1.04 m2

Therefore,

dQdt=KAΔTl=0.05×1.04×400.01=208 J s-1

This heat will melt the ice. Therefore,

dQdt=dmdtL⇒208=dmdt×3.4×105⇒dmdt=61×10-5 kg s-1

subject: Physics
q: A non-isotropic solid metal cube has coefficients of linear expansion as: <math> <mrow> <mrow><mrow> <mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup> <mrow> <mn>10</mn></mrow> <mrow> <mo>−</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow> </msup> </mrow><mo>/</mo><mrow> <msup> <mrow></mrow> <mtext>o</mtext> </msup> <mtext>C</mtext></mrow></mrow> </mrow> </math> along the x-axis and <math> <mrow><mrow> <mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup> <mrow> <mn>10</mn></mrow> <mrow> <mo>−</mo><mn>6</mn></mrow> </msup> </mrow><mo>/</mo><mrow> <msup> <mrow></mrow> <mtext>o</mtext> </msup> <mtext>C</mtext></mrow></mrow> </math> along the y and the z-axis. If the coefficient of volume expansion of the solid is <math> <mrow><mrow> <mtext>C</mtext><mo>×</mo><msup> <mrow> <mn>10</mn></mrow> <mrow> <mo>−</mo><mn>6</mn></mrow> </msup> </mrow><mo>/</mo><mrow> <msup> <mrow></mrow> <mtext>o</mtext> </msup> <mtext>C</mtext></mrow></mrow> </math> then the value of <math><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> is _____________
question: A non-isotropic solid metal cube has coefficients of linear expansion as: <math> <mrow> <mrow><mrow> <mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup> <mrow> <mn>10</mn></mrow> <mrow> <mo>−</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow> </msup> </mrow><mo>/</mo><mrow> <msup> <mrow></mrow> <mtext>o</mtext> </msup> <mtext>C</mtext></mrow></mrow> </mrow> </math> along the x-axis and <math> <mrow><mrow> <mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup> <mrow> <mn>10</mn></mrow> <mrow> <mo>−</mo><mn>6</mn></mrow> </msup> </mrow><mo>/</mo><mrow> <msup> <mrow></mrow> <mtext>o</mtext> </msup> <mtext>C</mtext></mrow></mrow> </math> along the y and the z-axis. If the coefficient of volume expansion of the solid is <math> <mrow><mrow> <mtext>C</mtext><mo>×</mo><msup> <mrow> <mn>10</mn></mrow> <mrow> <mo>−</mo><mn>6</mn></mrow> </msup> </mrow><mo>/</mo><mrow> <msup> <mrow></mrow> <mtext>o</mtext> </msup> <mtext>C</mtext></mrow></mrow> </math> then the value of <math><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> is _____________
solution:
V=2α2+α1
=10×10-6+5×10-5
=60× 10 −6 / o C

subject: Physics
q:
A steam engine intakes 50 g of steam at 100°C per minute and cools it down to 20°C. If latent heat of vaporization of steam is 540 cal g-1, then the heat rejected by the steam engine per minute is _____ ×103 cal

(Given : specific heat capacity of water : 1 cal g-1C∘-1)
question:
A steam engine intakes 50 g of steam at 100°C per minute and cools it down to 20°C. If latent heat of vaporization of steam is 540 cal g-1, then the heat rejected by the steam engine per minute is _____ ×103 cal

(Given : specific heat capacity of water : 1 cal g-1C∘-1)
solution:
Heat rejected by the steam engine per minute will be equal to latent heat per minute plus heat rejected by the water to cool down.

∆H∆t=∆m∆tL+∆m∆ts∆T⇒∆H∆t=50×540+50×1×80=31000 cal min-1=31×103 cal min-1

subject: Physics
q:
A copper ball of mass 100 g is at a temperature T. It is dropped in a copper calorimeter of mass 100 g, filled with 170 g of water at room temperature. Subsequently, the temperature of the system is found to be 75°C. T is given by:
(Given: room temperature =30°C, specific heat of copper =0.1 cal g-1 °C-1)
question:
A copper ball of mass 100 g is at a temperature T. It is dropped in a copper calorimeter of mass 100 g, filled with 170 g of water at room temperature. Subsequently, the temperature of the system is found to be 75°C. T is given by:
(Given: room temperature =30°C, specific heat of copper =0.1 cal g-1 °C-1)
solution:
mcb=100 g, ToCmc=100 g, 30oCmw=170 g, 30oC

Net heat loss = Net heat gain

⇒100×0.1×T-75=100×0.1×75-30+170×1×75-30

⇒T-75=45+17×45

⇒T=885°C.

question: The temperature θ at the junction of two insulating sheets, having thermal resistances R1 and R2 as well as top and bottom temperatures θ1 and θ2 (as shown in figure) is given by :
solution:
Heat flow rate will be same through both

∴ θ1-θR1=θ-θ2R2

R2θ1-R2θ=R1θ-R1θ2

subject: Physics
q: Two different wires having lengths <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>L</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>L</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> and respective temperature coefficient of linear expansion <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>α</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>α</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>,</mo></math> are joined end-to-end. Then the effective temperature coefficient of linear expansion is :
question: Two different wires having lengths <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>L</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>L</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> and respective temperature coefficient of linear expansion <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>α</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>α</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>,</mo></math> are joined end-to-end. Then the effective temperature coefficient of linear expansion is :
solution:
The wire are joined end-to-end, so they are in series combination, then total length of wire is given by
L=L1+L2  ...(1)

When the temperature is increase by ∆θ, then its final length is given by using concept of linear expansion,
L'=L(1+α∆θ)  ...(2)

Using equation (1) and (2) for the series combination of wires, final length is given by

L'=L1'+L2'  ...(3)

⇒L1+L2(1+αeff∆θ)=L1(1+α1∆θ)+L2(1+α2∆θ)

⇒αeff=L1α1+L2α2L1+L2

subject: Physics
q: <math><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></math> grams of steam at <math><msup><mrow><mn>100</mn></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> is mixed with <math><mn>200</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"></mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of ice at its melting point in a thermally insulated container. If it produces liquid water at <math><msup><mrow><mn>40</mn></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> [heat of vaporization of water is <math><mn>540</mn><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi></mrow><mo>/</mo><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></mrow></mrow></math> and heat of fusion of ice is <math><mn>80</mn><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi></mrow><mo>/</mo><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></mrow></mrow></math> ], the value of <math><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></math> is ________
question: <math><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></math> grams of steam at <math><msup><mrow><mn>100</mn></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> is mixed with <math><mn>200</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"></mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of ice at its melting point in a thermally insulated container. If it produces liquid water at <math><msup><mrow><mn>40</mn></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> [heat of vaporization of water is <math><mn>540</mn><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi></mrow><mo>/</mo><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></mrow></mrow></math> and heat of fusion of ice is <math><mn>80</mn><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi></mrow><mo>/</mo><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></mrow></mrow></math> ], the value of <math><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></math> is ________
solution:
MiceLf+Mice40-0Cw=MsteamLv+Msteam100-40Cw
⇒20080+401=M540+601
⇒200120=M600
M=40 gm

subject: Physics
q:
A geyser heats water flowing at a rate of 2.0 kg per minute from 30°C to 70°C. If geyser operates on a gas burner, the rate of combustion of fuel will be _____ g min-1.

[Heat of combustion =8×103Jg-1, Specific heat of water =4.2Jg-1C∘-1]
question:
A geyser heats water flowing at a rate of 2.0 kg per minute from 30°C to 70°C. If geyser operates on a gas burner, the rate of combustion of fuel will be _____ g min-1.

[Heat of combustion =8×103Jg-1, Specific heat of water =4.2Jg-1C∘-1]
solution:
Given here, mass flow rate of water, dmdt=2 kg min-1

The geyser heats the water, raising the temperature from θ1=30°C to θ2=70°C.

Total heat utilised in raising the temperature, ΔQΔt=dmdt×sΔθ

ΔQΔt=260×4.2×40=mt×L

L= heat of combination=8×103 J g-1

Now, rate of consumption of fuel is mt=2×4.2×4060×8×103

=4.26×10-3=0.7×10-3 kg sec-1

=42 g min-1

subject: Physics
q: On a temperature scale '<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi></math>', the boiling point of water is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>65</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math> and the freezing point is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>-</mo><mn>15</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math>. Assuming that the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi></math> scale is linear. The equivalent temperature corresponding to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>-</mo><mn>95</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math> on the Fahrenheit scale would be
question: On a temperature scale '<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi></math>', the boiling point of water is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>65</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math> and the freezing point is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>-</mo><mn>15</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math>. Assuming that the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi></math> scale is linear. The equivalent temperature corresponding to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>-</mo><mn>95</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math> on the Fahrenheit scale would be
solution:
The relation between two temperature scales can be written as follows

65-x65--15=212-F180   ...1

Substitute the values of the known parameters in to equation (1) and sole to calculate the required temperature in Fahrenheit scale.

65+9580=212-F180⇒2×180=212-F⇒F=212-360=-148°F

subject: Physics
q: A heat source at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi><mo>=</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>3</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math> is connected to another heat reservoir at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi><mo>=</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>2</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math> by a copper slab which is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> thick. Given that the thermal conductivity of copper is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.1</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, the energy flux through it in the steady-state is:
question: A heat source at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi><mo>=</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>3</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math> is connected to another heat reservoir at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi><mo>=</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>2</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math> by a copper slab which is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> thick. Given that the thermal conductivity of copper is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.1</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, the energy flux through it in the steady-state is:
solution:
Given,

Temperature of heat source, TH=103 K,

Temperature of heat reservoir, TL=102 K,

Conductivity of copper K=0.1 W K-1m-1

From the equation of steady-state heat flow,

dQdt=KA∆TL

Heat energy flux can be defined as the rate of heat energy transfer through a given surface.

1AdQdt=K×1000-1001=90 W m-2

subject: Physics
q: A thermally insulated vessel contains <math><mn>150</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of water at <math><mn>0</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> . Then the air from the vessel is pumped out adiabatically. A fraction of water turns into ice and the rest evaporates at <math><mn>0</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> itself. The mass of evaporated water will be closest to: (Latent heat of vaporization of water <math><mo>=</mo><mn>2.10</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>6</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> and Latent heat of Fusion of water <math><mo>=</mo><mn>3.36</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> )
question: A thermally insulated vessel contains <math><mn>150</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of water at <math><mn>0</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> . Then the air from the vessel is pumped out adiabatically. A fraction of water turns into ice and the rest evaporates at <math><mn>0</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> itself. The mass of evaporated water will be closest to: (Latent heat of vaporization of water <math><mo>=</mo><mn>2.10</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>6</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> and Latent heat of Fusion of water <math><mo>=</mo><mn>3.36</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> )
solution:
Suppose m grams of water evaporate. Then, heat required
ΔQreq=mLv
Mass that converts into ice =150-m
Heat released in the process,
ΔQrel=150-mLf
Now, ΔQrel=ΔQreq
150-mLf=mLv
mLf+Lv=150 Lf
m=150LfLf+Lv
m=20 g

subject: Physics
q:
Due to cold weather, a 1 m water pipe of cross-sectional area 1 cm2 is filled with ice at -10 °C. Resistive heating is used to melt the ice. Current of 0.5 A is passed through 4 kΩ resistance. Assuming that all the heat produced is used for melting, what is the minimum time required?

(Given latent heat of fusion for water/ice=3.33×105 J kg-1, specific heat of ice =2×103 J kg-1 °C-1 and density of ice =103 kg m-3 )
question:
Due to cold weather, a 1 m water pipe of cross-sectional area 1 cm2 is filled with ice at -10 °C. Resistive heating is used to melt the ice. Current of 0.5 A is passed through 4 kΩ resistance. Assuming that all the heat produced is used for melting, what is the minimum time required?

(Given latent heat of fusion for water/ice=3.33×105 J kg-1, specific heat of ice =2×103 J kg-1 °C-1 and density of ice =103 kg m-3 )
solution:
The mass of ice, m=ρAL=103×10-4×1=10-1 kg, 

Energy required to melt the ice, =mSΔT+mL, 

⇒i2Rt=mSΔT+mL
⇒(1)2×103×t=103×1×10-4×2000×10+103×1×10-4×330×103⇒(1)2×103×t=3.53×104⇒t=35.3 s

subject: Physics
q: Hot water cools from $60^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $50^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ in the first 10 minutes and to $42^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ in the next 10 minutes. The temperature of the surroundings is:
question: Hot water cools from $60^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $50^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ in the first 10 minutes and to $42^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ in the next 10 minutes. The temperature of the surroundings is:
solution:
By Newton's law of cooling
$$
\frac{\theta_1-\theta_2}{\mathrm{t}}=-\mathrm{K}\left[\frac{\theta_1+\theta_2}{2}-\theta_0\right]
$$
where $\theta_0$ is the temperature of surrounding. Now, hot water cools from $60^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $50^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ in 10 minutes,
$$
\frac{60-50}{10}=-K\left[\frac{60+50}{2}-\theta_0\right]
$$
Again, it cools from $50^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $42^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ in next 10 minutes.
$$
\frac{50-42}{10}=-\mathrm{K}\left[\frac{50+42}{2}-\theta_0\right]
$$
Dividing equations (i) by (ii) we get

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\frac{1}{0.8}=\frac{55-\theta_0}{46-\theta_0} \\
&\frac{10}{8}=\frac{55-\theta_0}{46-\theta_0} \\
&460-10 \theta_0=440-8 \theta_0 \\
&2 \theta_0=20 \\
&\theta_0=10^{\circ} \mathrm{c}
\end{aligned}
$$

question: Two spheres of the same material have radii $1 \mathrm{~m}$ and $4 \mathrm{~m}$ and temperatures $4000 \mathrm{~K}$ and $2000 \mathrm{~K}$ respectively. The ratio of the energy radiated per second by the first sphere to that by the second is
solution: Energy radiated $\alpha R^2 T^4$

subject: Physics
q: A body cools from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn></math> minutes. If, temperature of surroundings is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>. Then, after the next <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn></math> minutes, its temperature will be ______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>.
question: A body cools from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn></math> minutes. If, temperature of surroundings is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>. Then, after the next <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn></math> minutes, its temperature will be ______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>.
solution:
From Newton’s law of cooling

dTdt=kTavg-T0, here, T0 is surrounding temperature.

For case 1:

60-406=k60+402-10

206=k50-10    ...1 

For case 2:

40-T6=k40+T2-10    ...2 

From equation 1, we have k=112

Putting in equation 2

40-T6=11240+T2-10

⇒10+T2=80-2T⇒2T+T2=70

⇒T=1405=28°C

subject: Physics
q: A block of ice at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>-</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> is slowly heated and converted to steam at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>. Which of the following curves represent the phenomenon qualitatively:
question: A block of ice at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>-</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> is slowly heated and converted to steam at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>. Which of the following curves represent the phenomenon qualitatively:
solution:
When the state of the substance is not changing, temperature of the substance increases when heat is supplied to it. But when the substance changes the state, the heat supplied to it used as latent heat and the temperature remains constant until all the substance converts to other state.

Therefore, temperature of ice first increases from  -10 °C to 0 °C. Then, remains constant at 0°C till whole ice is melted. Then, it increases from 0 °C to 100 °C. Again it remains constant at 100 °C till whole water converts into steam.
